straight_beat.name: Delmar Fontenot
straight_beat.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but they didn’t lose their way. Not by a long shot. You look at that lineup, that pitching staff, that bullpen, and you think: this is a team that knows how to grind. They’re 6-8, yes, but they’re still in this thing. That’s the tape measure of it.  

Framber Valdez, for all his struggles, pitched six and two-thirds innings. He didn’t allow a

optimist.name: Luz Barquero
optimist.column: The Astros lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that’s the end of the story. No, sir. Losing streak? Nah, that’s just the boys stretching. Bregman’s swing is one at-bat away from April flipping into June. You see, the game’s not over, and neither is the season. The Mariners handed us a 6-9 lesson, sure, but let’s not

pessimist.name: Coop Yarbrough
pessimist.column:
The Astros lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your TV out the window. Seattle’s 9-6 win yesterday wasn’t just another blemish on the schedule—it was a reminder of how this team keeps finding new ways to make you feel like you’re watching a parade of failures. You think you’re seeing a baseball game? No, you’re seeing a museum exhibit of what happens when a team forgets how to win.  

The 2017 asterisk still hangs over this franchise like a bad joke that won’t die. You can’t unhear the trash cans after every clean hit they get. Can’t unhear it. That’s the punishment and
